body:
**01. fabrication & qc**\
The bare boards arrived with zero electrical defects. I did notice some minor solder mask anomalies on a few of the high-density IC footprints. This seems to be a manufacturing edge case, likely due to me pushing the pad clearance tolerances too close to JLC's limits, but the copper pads themselves are perfectly intact. Overall, the boards are perfectly functional, just with some surface defects.

**02. esp32 reflow**\
Without access to a reflow oven, I used a hot plate to mount the ESP32. The process was straightforward: I tinned the pads on both the module and the PCB, applied heat, and let surface tension pull the board precisely onto the underlying footprint.

**03. dfn packages & hot air**\
This was my first time soldering DFN packages. I applied solder paste using a stencil and reflowed the components with a hot air station, which made the process surprisingly simple. I initially considered using an oven for this step, but to avoid thermally stressing the already-mounted ESP32, I figured that localized hot air was the safest route.

**04. logistics + next steps**\
During assembly, I realized the DigiKey order was missing a decoupling capacitor for the top battery-checker circuit. They shipped a replacement immediately, but it arrived after I had already moved off campus. Since it’s strictly a decoupling cap, I figure that I can safely proceed with initial power-on testing without it.

Overall, the assembly went incredibly smoothly. I am currently waiting on a final shipment of connectors from BetaFPV, and then the board will be ready for full validation!
summary: The bare boards from JLC arrived flawlessly, despite a few small hiccups with the DigiKey order. One board is now fully assembled. Components were soldered on using a hot plate, an iron, and a hot air station.
title: Boards arrived & Soldered
